name: Las Vegas (manganese area)
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Las Vegas for manganese deposits and oxidized mineral specimens. Personal use collection allowed year-round with no permit required.
description:
This BLM location near Las Vegas offers accessible rockhounding for manganese oxide minerals and associated iron oxides in the Mojave Desert. The site sits within the broader Basin and Range physiography where Tertiary-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity created zones of metal oxide mineralization. Collectors find black manganese oxides, limonite staining, and quartz specimens in weathered outcrops and desert washes. The open desert terrain allows easy walking access to multiple collecting areas within short distances from parking. This is an ideal beginner-friendly location for finding oxidized mineral specimens without the cost of fee dig sites.

The manganese deposits here formed during periods of groundwater circulation through fault zones and volcanic rocks, where dissolved manganese precipitated as oxides coating fracture surfaces and filling small cavities. Desert weathering has exposed these deposits on hillsides and in wash banks where they are visible as dark, heavy mineral crusts and dendrites on host rock. The location is popular with mineral collectors seeking specimens for study and collections.

Expect to find loose specimens weathered from parent rock, small nodules, and coating patterns on quartz and feldspar. The desert floor is covered with scattered collecting material, making this a productive site for those willing to spend time examining washes and exposed slopes. No special equipment is required beyond a basic rock hammer and collection bag.
history: This area lies within the Mojave Desert portion of the Basin and Range province where Miocene-age hydrothermal circulation through fractured volcanic rocks deposited manganese oxides and iron oxides in fault zones and fracture networks. Desert weathering over millions of years has exposed these deposits at the surface, creating the accessible collecting site that exists today.
tips: Search the bottoms and banks of desert washes where water erosion has concentrated heavier mineral specimens; manganese oxides accumulate in these natural collection zones. Look for black, heavy specimens with a dull to metallic luster - real manganese oxide feels noticeably heavier than quartz of the same size. Bring plenty of water and sunscreen even in winter months; the open desert offers no shade and heat reflection from light-colored rocks is intense. Early morning or late afternoon visits are most comfortable and provide better lighting for spotting dark specimens against lighter desert soil.
terrain_notes: Desert terrain at approximately 2,000-2,500 feet elevation with sparse creosote and bursage scrub vegetation typical of the Mojave Desert. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is typically under 0.5 miles. Terrain is relatively flat to gently rolling with occasional washes and low ridges. Footing is sandy with loose rock and scattered small boulders; sturdy hiking boots are recommended. No significant elevation gain required.
best_season: October - April. Desert heat makes collecting uncomfortable May through September, with temperatures regularly exceeding 100 degrees Fahrenheit. Winter months (November-February) offer the most pleasant conditions with daytime temperatures in the 50s-60s, though evening temperatures can drop significantly. Spring (March-April) and fall (October) are also excellent collecting windows.
written_directions: From Las Vegas city center, take US 95 North approximately 35 miles toward Beatty. Watch for BLM access roads on the west side of US 95 in the Mohave County area around mile marker 75-85. Pull off at the designated BLM parking area or rough pullouts; these are typically unsigned dirt areas with evidence of previous collecting activity. From the pullout, walk northwest into the desert wash system where manganese specimens are concentrated. GPS coordinate 35.743073, -114.387996 marks the core collecting area; use a GPS device or smartphone GPS to navigate to the exact location.
road_conditions: US 95 is well-maintained highway. Access roads to BLM parking areas are unpaved dirt, passable for standard passenger vehicles in dry conditions. High-clearance or 4WD may be needed after recent rain.
parking_notes: Informal BLM parking pullouts along access roads. No developed parking lot. Space for 3-5 vehicles at most pullouts. No facilities, no water, no restrooms.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or surface disturbance. Hand tools only (rock hammer, chisel, hand shovel). All collecting must be from surface or naturally weathered material; do not dig into intact outcrops. Pack out all trash. Respect any existing claims or marked areas.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only (rock hammer, chisel)
No digging into intact outcrops
Collect surface material only
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Las Vegas, approximately 35 miles south. Full services including gas, water, food, and supplies available. No services at the collecting site.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat desert terrain accessible to most visitors. Uneven sandy footing with scattered small rocks requires sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair accessibility. Walking distances are short (under 0.5 miles from parking to main collecting areas). Limited to no shade available. Not suitable for those with heat sensitivity or mobility limitations.

faq.question: What manganese specimens can I find at the Las Vegas BLM rockhounding area?
faq.answer: This location is known for manganese oxide minerals, particularly black manganese oxides (pyrolusite and other oxides), often found coating quartz or feldspar, and manganese dendrites creating tree-like patterns on host rock. You will also find associated iron oxides (limonite) staining specimens. Most pieces are small to medium-sized surface specimens that have weathered from parent rock and accumulated in washes. Specimens are collectible for mineral study and hobby collections.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding near Las Vegas on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on this BLM site near Las Vegas. Collection is allowed for personal use only under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). You are limited to 25 pounds per day. This free access makes it an excellent option compared to fee dig sites, though the quantities and specimen sizes may be smaller.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at the Las Vegas manganese site?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal, with the most pleasant conditions November through February when daytime temperatures range from 50-65 degrees. Avoid May through September when desert heat regularly exceeds 100 degrees Fahrenheit and collecting becomes uncomfortable and unsafe. Spring (March-April) and fall (October) are also excellent windows with moderate temperatures and good collecting conditions.

faq.question: How far is the Las Vegas manganese rockhounding area from the city and what driving directions apply?
faq.answer: The site is approximately 35 miles north of Las Vegas city center. Take US 95 North toward Beatty. Watch for BLM access roads on the west side of US 95 around mile marker 75-85 in Mohave County. Turn onto designated BLM access roads and park at unsigned pullouts showing evidence of previous collecting. Use GPS coordinates 35.743073, -114.387996 to navigate to the core collecting area. The drive from Las Vegas takes about 45 minutes to an hour.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need to bring for rockhounding at this BLM site?
faq.answer: Bring a basic rock hammer, chisel, and hand shovel for surface collecting. Hand tools are required; motorized equipment and significant digging are not permitted. Pack a sturdy collection bag, plenty of water (at least 2-3 liters per person), sunscreen, and a wide-brimmed hat. A GPS device or smartphone is helpful to locate the exact collecting areas. Since there are no facilities at the site, bring everything you need for your visit. Sturdy hiking boots are essential for uneven sandy terrain.

faq.question: Is the Las Vegas manganese rockhounding area suitable for beginners and children?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent beginner-friendly location. The terrain is relatively flat with short walking distances from parking (typically under 0.5 miles to productive areas). No special skills or expensive equipment are required. Children age 6 and older can participate comfortably. The main considerations are desert heat, which requires adequate water and sun protection, and the remote location. Always supervise children and ensure adequate hydration. It is a great introduction to rockhounding without the cost of fee dig sites.

meta_title: Las Vegas Manganese Rockhounding - BLM, No Permit
meta_description: Free rockhounding on BLM land near Las Vegas for manganese oxides and mineral specimens. Personal use, no permit required, 25 lbs/day limit. October-April.
